content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 33 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), PNEUMA (dynamic) | **Base text:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 118r–119v (LC PDF pp. 234–235) | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus, pp. 165–166

*Note: Chapter 33 contains both a poetic frame (vv. 1–5, 26–29) and individual tribal blessings (vv. 6–25). The Leningrad Codex sets the opening and closing sections in a distinctive poetic layout. The tribal blessings are written in running poetry with verse divisions marked. This translation preserves the bicolon structure throughout the poetic sections.*

---

**1** This is the blessing [berakhah] with which Moses the man of God blessed the sons of Israel before his death.

**2** And he said:

The LORD came from Sinai
    and dawned upon them from Seir;
he shone forth from Mount Paran,
    he came from the ten thousands of holy ones,
with a fiery law [esh dat] at his right hand for them.

**3**
Yes, he loves the peoples —
    all his holy ones are in your hand.
And they bowed down at your feet,
    each receiving of your words.

**4**
Moses commanded us an instruction [torah],
    a possession for the assembly of Jacob.

**5**
And he became king in Jeshurun [Yeshurun],
    when the heads of the people were gathered,
all the tribes of Israel together.

---

**Reuben**

**6** Let Reuben live and not die,
    but let his men be few.

---

**Judah**

**7** And this he said for Judah:

Hear, O LORD, the voice of Judah,
    and bring him in to his people.
With his hands he contended for himself,
    and you shall be a help against his adversaries.

---

**Levi**

**8** And of Levi he said:

Your Thummim and your Urim belong to your devout man [chasid],
    whom you tested at Massah
and with whom you contended at the waters of Meribah.

**9**
Who said of his father and his mother, "I have not seen them,"
    and his brothers he did not acknowledge
and his sons he did not know —
    for they kept your word
and your covenant they guarded.

**10**
They shall teach your ordinances to Jacob
    and your instruction [torah] to Israel;
they shall place incense before you
    and whole burnt offerings upon your altar.

**11**
Bless, O LORD, his strength,
    and accept the work of his hands;
crush the loins of those who rise against him,
    of those who hate him, that they rise no more.

---

**Benjamin**

**12** Of Benjamin he said:

The beloved of the LORD dwells in safety by him;
    he shields him all the day long,
and he dwells between his shoulders.

---

**Joseph**

**13** And of Joseph he said:

Blessed by the LORD be his land,
    with the choicest gifts of heaven above,
with the deep that crouches below,

**14**
and with the choicest fruits of the sun
    and the precious yield of the months,

**15**
and with the finest produce of the ancient mountains
    and the precious things of the everlasting hills,

**16**
and with the choicest gifts of the earth and its fullness,
    and the favor of him who dwelt in the bush [shokhni seneh].
Let these come upon the head of Joseph,
    upon the crown of the one set-apart among his brothers.

**17**
A firstborn bull — his majesty is his!
    And his horns are the horns of a wild ox [re'em];
with them he shall gore the peoples,
    all of them, to the ends of the earth —
and these are the myriads of Ephraim
    and these are the thousands of Manasseh.

---

**Zebulun and Issachar**

**18** And of Zebulun he said:

Rejoice, Zebulun, in your going out,
    and Issachar, in your tents.

**19**
They shall call peoples to the mountain;
    there they will offer righteous sacrifices;
for they shall draw from the abundance of the seas
    and the hidden treasures of the sand.

---

**Gad**

**20** And of Gad he said:

Blessed is he who makes Gad large!
    As a lion he crouches and tears at the arm — even the crown.

**21**
And he chose the best of the land for himself,
    for there the commander's portion was reserved;
and he came with the heads of the people —
    the righteousness of the LORD he carried out,
and his ordinances with Israel.

---

**Dan**

**22** And of Dan he said:

Dan is a lion's cub
    that leaps from Bashan.

---

**Naphtali**

**23** And of Naphtali he said:

O Naphtali, satisfied with favor
    and full of the blessing of the LORD —
possess the west and the south.

---

**Asher**

**24** And of Asher he said:

Most blessed of sons is Asher!
    Let him be pleasing to his brothers
and let him dip his foot in oil.

**25**
Iron and bronze are your bars,
    and as your days, so shall your strength be.

---

**Closing Doxology**

**26**
There is none like the God of Jeshurun [Yeshurun],
    who rides through the heavens to your help,
through the clouds in his majesty.

**27**
The eternal God is your dwelling place,
    and underneath are the everlasting arms.
And he drove out the enemy before you
    and said: "Destroy!"

**28**
So Israel dwelt in safety,
    Jacob alone — the spring of Jacob —
in a land of grain and new wine,
    even his heavens drip dew.

**29**
How blessed are you, O Israel! Who is like you,
    a people saved by the LORD?
He is the shield of your help
    and the sword of your triumph.
Your enemies shall come cringing before you,
    and you shall tread upon their backs.

[Ashreikhah Yisra'el — How blessed are you, O Israel!]

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

**V.2 — "fiery law" [esh dat]:** The phrase אֵשׁ דָּת (esh dat) is a hapax legomenon of uncertain etymology (possibly Aramaic dat = decree/law). EMET preserves "fiery law" as the lectio difficilior. The LXX reads "angels with him" from its Vorlage. SOPHIA follows the MT: "a fiery law at his right hand for them."

**V.2 — "ten thousands of holy ones":** מֵרִבְבֹת קֹדֶשׁ (me-rivvot qodesh) — the divine entourage: myriads of the heavenly host attending the LORD's theophanic march. EMET reads "myriads of holy ones" and LOGOS reads "ten thousands of holy ones." "Ten thousands" is the literal value of rivvot; LOGOS is adopted.

**V.5 — "he became king":** The ambiguous referent (the LORD or Moses?) is preserved with "he" — both agents note the ambiguity. SOPHIA does not resolve it, as both readings are theologically defensible and the Hebrew leaves it open.

**V.8 — Thummim before Urim:** The unusual order (Thummim first, then Urim) is the MT reading and is preserved. This reversed order from the more common "Urim and Thummim" is a distinctive archaic feature of this old poem.

**V.16 — shokhni seneh:** "Him who dwelt in the bush" — a direct allusion to Exodus 3 (the burning bush theophany), here employed as a divine epithet for the Joseph blessing. PNEUMA's fuller "burning bush" is adopted in preference to the terse "bush" alone.

**V.17 — re'em:** The re'em (rendered "wild ox" with gloss) is the aurochs (Bos primigenius), not a unicorn. The LXX's μονοκέρωτος ("unicorn") is the source of the KJV error.

**V.20 — lion imagery for Gad:** EMET's "lioness" vs. LOGOS/PNEUMA's "lion" — the Hebrew לָבִיא (lavi) is a lion (gender-inclusive in some uses, specifically female in others). See disagreement log.

**V.27 — "everlasting arms":** תַּחַת זְרֹעֹת עוֹלָם (tachat zero'ot olam) — "underneath are the arms of eternity." The phrase "everlasting arms" has become one of the most celebrated in all of Deuteronomy. SOPHIA adopts it as the established rendering.

**V.29 — ashreikhah Yisra'el:** The climactic beatitude closing the poem. "How blessed are you, O Israel!" preserves the exclamatory second-person address. The final image of enemies cringing/cowering and Israel treading on their backs forms the triumphant close of the Blessing.

— *SOPHIA*
